summary: Martin Fowler identifies common presentation anti-patterns that reduce the effectiveness of conference talks. He argues against putting sentences on slides, as they compete with the speaker's voice for the audience's attention. He criticizes repetitive elements like corporate logos on every slide, unnecessary pointer movements that should be built into slides, backtracking through slide decks, and mandatory slide titles. However, he concludes that content quality matters most—good content can overcome poor presentation technique, while great slides cannot save weak content.
key_points: Sentences on slides force audiences to parse written and spoken words simultaneously, breaking the flow of attention
Repetitive elements like logos and legal text either get tuned out (making them useless) or distract the audience
If you repeatedly point to the same things or backtrack to earlier slides, your slide sequence needs restructuring
Slide titles should be questioned rather than assumed mandatory—removing them often improves the visual channel
Content quality ultimately trumps presentation technique; audiences forgive poor slides if the ideas are interesting
money_quotes: Reading sentences breaks that up as the audience is both trying to parse the written word and the spoken words at the same time.
The audience either tunes out the floodmark (which makes them useless) or is distracted by them (which is worse).
Are your slides really so amazing that you'll lose a fortune if someone copies them?
If you find yourself going back to a previous slide, that indicates the slide sequence isn't right.
If the content isn't solid, or you don't really care about it, then even outstanding slidecraft isn't going to save you.
Conversely an audience will forgive all sorts of crummy presentation techniques if you have interesting things to say.
key_insight: Slides should serve as a visual channel that complements rather than competes with the speaker's words, but ultimately no amount of slide optimization can substitute for having something worthwhile to say.
